coredata.dc:title: Design and preparation of a fluorescent molecularly imprinted membrane for the selective detection of pepsin enzyme as a biomarker for gastroesophageal reflux disease
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Mostafa, Aya M.
Barton, Stephen J.
Wren, Stephen P.
Barker, James
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer membrane (FMIM) has been developed for the selective binding and qualitative detection of pepsin enzyme, a biomarker indicative of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). This study utilises the high selectivity offered by molecular imprinting techniques to capture pepsin enzyme within complex biological matrices, such as human saliva. Additionally, fluorescent carbon dots integrated into the membrane matrix provide instant visual detection of pepsin. Various combinations of functional monomers and cross-linkers were systematically evaluated to investigate their impact on the binding capacity and mechanical stability of the resultant FMIMs. The optimum performance was achieved with a mixture of two hydrophilic monomers, namely N-(hydroxymethyl)acrylamide and acrylamide, in conjunction with N,N-methylenebis(acrylamide) as the cross-linking agent. The developed FMIM demonstrated a binding capacity of 21.56 mg g-1, surpassing that of the fluorescent non-imprinted membrane (FNIM) at 8.49 mg g-1. Moreover, the binding of FMIM to pepsin was tested against other competitor enzymes to verify its selectivity. Furthermore, comprehensive characterisation of both FMIM and FNIM was conducted using various analytical techniques to ensure their structural integrity and functionality. Ultimately, the developed FMIM exhibited effective binding of pepsin in standard solutions and samples, enabling enrichment and visual detection of the biomarker enzyme.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent membrane imprinted polymers
Fluorescent biosensors
Pepsin
Biomarkers
Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent molecularly imprinted membrane (FMIM)
Characterisation of the developed FMIM and FNIM
Characterisation of the developed FMIM and FNIM
Effect of pH on fluorescence response
Comparison of this method with other reported MIMs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the synthesis process for carbon dots (CDs) and the fluorescent molecularly imprinted membrane (FMIM) highlighting key steps and reactions involved.
The light microscope images of a) FMIM, b) FNIM, and the fluorescent microscope images of c) FMIM under 4x magnification, d) FMIM under 16x magnification.
Infrared (IR) spectra comparing the chemical profiles of CDs, FMIM, and FNIM, illustrating the distinct functional groups.

Thermal analysis results for FMIM and FNIM: (a) and (b) show TGA thermographs illustrating the thermal stability and decomposition behaviour of FMIM and FNIM, respectively; (c) and (d) present DSC thermographs, highlighting the thermal transitions and specific heat capacities of FMIM and FNIM, respectively.

Analysis of binding interactions: (a) Binding kinetics curves for FMIM and FNIM, showing the rate and extent of binding over time; (b) Binding isotherms for FMIM and FNIM, depicting the equilibrium binding capacities.

Influence of pH on FMIM fluorescence: (Blue) Fluorescence emission of FMIM across different pH levels in the presence of pepsin; (Black) Fluorescence emission of FMIM across different pH levels in the absence of pepsin, highlighting the impact of pH and enzyme presence on the fluorescence behaviour.
Binding kinetics parameters for FMIM and FNIM applying pseudo first and pseudo second binding orders

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: K1 and K2 are the first and second order rate constants respectively, Qe is the quantity of pepsin adsorbed per gram of polymer at equilibrium, and R2 is the linearity coefficient.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Adsorption isotherm parameters of FMIM and FNIM applying two models.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: KL and KF are the Langmuir constant and Freundlich constant respectively, Qmax is the theoretical maximum adsorbed concentration, RL is the separation factor (1/1+Ceq.KL), n is the variation trend coefficient for the adsorption isotherm, and R2 is the linearity coefficient.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison between this work and other reported molecularly imprinted membranes.

row.entry: l-cysteine-capped Mn2+-doped ZnS quantum dots, Am and MBA.
Am, 4-vinylpyridine, methacrylic acid, and ethyleneglycol dimethacrylate.
Methyl methacrylate and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate.

head.ce:title: Design and preparation of a fluorescent molecularly imprinted membrane for the selective detection of pepsin enzyme as a biomarker for gastroesophageal reflux disease

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston upon Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Life Sciences, Pharmacy and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston upon Thames, London KT1 2EE, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, 71526, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, 71526, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer membrane (FMIM) has been developed for the selective binding and qualitative detection of pepsin enzyme, a biomarker indicative of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). This study utilises the high selectivity offered by molecular imprinting techniques to capture pepsin enzyme within complex biological matrices, such as human saliva. Additionally, fluorescent carbon dots integrated into the membrane matrix provide instant visual detection of pepsin. Various combinations of functional monomers and cross-linkers were systematically evaluated to investigate their impact on the binding capacity and mechanical stability of the resultant FMIMs. The optimum performance was achieved with a mixture of two hydrophilic monomers, namely N-(hydroxymethyl)acrylamide and acrylamide, in conjunction with N,N-methylenebis(acrylamide) as the cross-linking agent. The developed FMIM demonstrated a binding capacity of 21.56 mg g-1, surpassing that of the fluorescent non-imprinted membrane (FNIM) at 8.49 mg g-1. Moreover, the binding of FMIM to pepsin was tested against other competitor enzymes to verify its selectivity. Furthermore, comprehensive characterisation of both FMIM and FNIM was conducted using various analytical techniques to ensure their structural integrity and functionality. Ultimately, the developed FMIM exhibited effective binding of pepsin in standard solutions and samples, enabling enrichment and visual detection of the biomarker enzyme.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are synthetic smart materials designed to selectively bind to a target molecule or analyte based on its specific molecular structure [1]. MIPs are created through a process called molecular imprinting, where functional monomers are polymerised around the template molecule to create cavities or binding sites that mimic the shape, size, and functional groups’ orientation of the target molecule. Once the template molecule is removed, the resulting polymer retains these imprinted sites, allowing it to selectively recognise and bind the target molecule in different complex matrices [2].

Among the various types of MIPs, molecularly imprinted polymer membranes (MIMs) present unique advantages over traditional bulk MIPs. Unlike bulk MIPs, which are typically in powder form, MIMs are thin, film-like structures with binding sites distributed uniformly across their surface. This thin film morphology offers numerous benefits, the most important of which is the increased accessibility of binding sites and enhanced mass transfer kinetics. One notable advantage of MIMs, particularly relevant for larger analytes such as proteins, is their superior binding capacity. The thin, porous nature of MIMs allows for a larger surface area, accommodating a higher density of binding sites per unit area. This increased binding capacity enables MIMs to efficiently capture larger analytes, leading to enhanced sensitivity and detection limits in analytical applications [3]. Additionally, MIMs offer several other advantages, including low preparation costs and convenient operation. Their simple synthesis and scalability make them cost-effective options for large-scale applications. Furthermore, the ease of use and robustness make MIMs practical choices for various analytical and separation tasks [4,5].

Recent advancements in optical sensing have significantly benefited from the development of nanomaterials, which offer enhanced sensitivity and selectivity. Various studies have explored the potential of different nanomaterials for optical sensing applications. For instance, single walled carbon nanotubes were utilised for their near IR fluorescence emission which was augmented through the use of Ag and Au nanoparticles to develop an optical sensor for detection of ferrocyanide [6]. Similarly, tryptophan functionalised Ag nanoparticles were employed in a colorimetric sensor for the determination of magnesium ions with high sensitivity and with differentiation from calcium ions [7]. The work by S. Mirjalili [8], et al, highlighted the integration of antibody functionalised gold nanorods in clinical diagnostics, emphasising their effectiveness in real-time monitoring. Recent research also includes advancements in optical sensor technology for cancer detection through biomarkers tracking [9]. These studies illustrate the diverse applications and ongoing innovations in nanomaterial-based optical sensors [10,11].

Fluorescent membrane molecularly imprinted polymers (FMIMs) represent a novel approach in optical sensor development by incorporating fluorescent nanoparticles like quantum dots (QDs) or carbon dots (CDs) into their matrices. This integration not only enhances the selectivity of the MIPs but also offers a fluorescent detection of the target analyte [12,13].

CDs are emerging carbon-based nanomaterials renowned for their unique fluorescence properties, making them promising candidates for various applications. Their fluorescence originates from the quantum confinement effect and surface state emissions, enabling tuneable emission wavelengths [14]. CDs offer several advantages, including low toxicity, biocompatibility, and environmentally friendly synthesis routes using abundant carbon sources. Their facile synthesis methods, often involving one-step processes, make them cost-effective and suitable for large-scale production [15,16]. One promising application of CDs involves integrating them with MIPs, where CDs serve as visual detectors upon binding with target analytes. This integration enhances the selectivity and sensitivity of MIP-based sensors, offering a versatile platform for rapid and visual detection of various targets in complex matrices [17].

Given the numerous benefits associated with FMIMs, they have been selected in this work as the technique of choice for the imprinting of pepsin enzyme as a target. Pepsin enzyme is a digestive enzyme of molecular weight 34 KDa that aids in the digestion of proteins in the stomach [18]. However, recent research has confirmed that elevated levels of pepsin in human saliva can be used as a biomarker for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) [19–21]. This has led to the development of extraction and quantitation methods for salivary pepsin using molecular imprinting techniques [22,23]. Nevertheless, there are additional advantages that could be offered by FMIMs in the binding and detection of salivary pepsin in comparison to the conventional bulk imprinting methods.

A review of the literature revealed some earlier studies that applied MIPs for pepsin measurement. In a 2013 study by Pluhar et al., MIPs were developed using a mini emulsion imprinting technique, resulting in small particle size and increased surface area for binding [24]. However, optimisation was lacking for various synthesis elements, with few monomers tested. A later study by Pluhar further evaluated binding performance of the developed MIPs, yet it lacked optimisation and biological sample application [25]. Two other methods utilising MIPs in an ELISA format for pepsin detection were developed. A method published in 2018 by Piletska et al. employed magnetic MIPs immobilised in microtitre plate wells [26], while a more recent method by Garcia et al. utilised microtitre plates modified with magnetic inserts and covalently bound pepsin to magnetic nanoparticles [27]. Nonetheless, to the extent of our knowledge, no FMIMs were developed for the binding of pepsin enzyme, which encouraged us to explore this application further.

In this study, a novel hydrophilic FMIM was developed and tailored specifically for the selective binding of the pepsin enzyme. The goal was to create a fluorescent membrane capable of efficiently extracting and detecting pepsin in different solutions, while offering several distinct advantages over existing methods. One key advantage of our FMIM is its hydrophilic nature, which ensures compatibility with water-based matrices commonly encountered in biological samples. Additionally, the integration of CDs in the FMIM allows for fluorescent detection of pepsin that can be easily visualised under a UV light.

N-(Hydroxymethyl)acrylamide (NHMAm) 98 %, acrylamide (Am) 99 %, acrylic acid (AA) (99 %), N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm) 99 %, N,N′-methylenebis(acrylamide) (MBA) 99 %, ammonium persulphate (APS) 98 %, N,N,N′,N′-tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) 99.5 %, deionised water, sodium chloride 99.5 %, human pepsin, human lipase, human amylase, ɣglobulin, thrombin and Corning® microscope slides were all procured from Sigma Aldrich, UK. Folic acid (95%), N-[tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl]acrylamide (THMMAm) 99%, sulphuric acid AR, hydrogen peroxide 30 %, and Parafilm™ laboratory wrapping film were all purchased from Thermo Fisher, UK .

UV spectra were acquired using a Cary UV-Vis compact spectrophotometer with Cary UV Workstation™ software version 1.0.1284 (Agilent, UK). A UV lamp (UVitec, UK) was used for visual detection of fluorescence at wavelength 365 nm. Thermogravimetric analysis was conducted using a TGA 550 instrument (TA Instruments, UK) with Trios™ software version 10.00 (UK), while differential scanning calorimetric assays (DSC) were performed on a DSC25 series instrument (TA Instruments, UK) with Trios™ software version 5.4.0.300. Infrared analysis utilised a Nicolet iS5 Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) instrument operated with OMNIC™ software (UK). Images of the membrane surface were taken using an Olympus BX-51 light microscope with a Micropublisher 3.3 digital camera and Image-Pro Express capture software (Olympus, Tokyo Japan). Fluorescent images were taken via a Nikon 80i fluorescence microscope using mercury lamp illumination, Digital Sight DS-U1 camera and NIS elements capture software (Nikon labs, Toyo, Japan). Data processing and graph plotting were carried out using Origin™ 8.5 software (Origin Lab Corporation, North Hampton, USA).

Ultra fluorescent CDs were synthesised according to a reported method from folic acid as a carbon source [28]. Folic acid (50 mg) was dispersed in 7 mL of double deionised water by ultrasonication. The solution was then transferred into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 240 °C for 6 h. The resulting yellowish solution was filtered through a 0.45 µm filter to remove undissolved reactants and stored in amber coloured containers to prevent any photobleaching.

Prior to spotting the FMIM mixture, the glass slides were immersed in a mixture of H2SO4/H2O2 for 24 h to clean and activate their surfaces. Subsequently, they were washed three times with double deionised water in an ultrasonic bath to remove any residual chemicals or impurities. Following this, the slides were dried under nitrogen to ensure a clean and dry surface, which was crucial for the even and smooth deposition of the FMIM mixture. This process was undertaken to enhance the adhesion and stability of the FMIM on the glass slides, thereby improving their performance for subsequent applications [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent molecularly imprinted membrane (FMIM)
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the protein nature of the target pepsin enzyme, its limited solubility in water, and its prevalence in aqueous matrices [19], the synthesis of hydrophilic FMIMs was necessary. Various hydrophilic monomers were assessed to fabricate an optimal FMIM that achieves the highest possible binding capacity while maintaining superior mechanical stability and reproducibility in aqueous environments [30]. Achieving the ideal balance between optimal binding and robust mechanical stability posed a challenge. Various monomers, such as NHMAm, THMMAm, NIPAm, AA, and Am were assessed individually, and in combination, to determine the most effective combination for achieving both superior binding capacity and mechanical resilience. Following thorough testing, the synthesis procedure was optimised by employing a mixture of NHMAm (1.24 mmol) and Am (1.76 mmol) as functional monomers, along with MBA (0.2 mmol) as the cross-linker. The functional monomers and cross-linker were dissolved in 3 mL of phosphate buffer (0.02 mol L-1, pH 7) containing 25 µL of CDs solution. Subsequently, 0.15 µmol of pepsin was introduced into the mixture, followed by sonication, and stirring for 1 h to facilitate preassembly between the target molecule and the monomers. After preassembly, 100 µL of (10 % w/v) APS and 5 µL of TEMED were added followed by immediate spotting of the mixture on the activated glass slides. The solution was then covered carefully with square pieces of Parafilm™ cut into 4 cm2 squares and left to polymerise overnight. The same procedure was carried out to prepare the fluorescent non-imprinted membrane (FNIM) without the addition of the target pepsin.

The resulting films were carefully cut in rectangles (2 × 1 cm), avoiding the dry edges, and washed with double deionised water for 1 h to remove residuals of the starting materials. Following this, the membranes were soaked overnight in a 0.5 mol L-1 sodium chloride solution to remove the target from the binding sites of FMIM and then washed again with double deionised water twice.

The binding capacity of the developed FMIMs was evaluated using a batch adsorption method at room temperature (25 °C ± 2 °C). Each FMIM and its corresponding FNIM were incubated with 10 mL of a 0.5 mg mL-1 pepsin solution in water for 4 hours with gentle stirring. After incubation, the membranes were removed, and the remaining pepsin concentration in solution was determined by UV spectrometry to calculate the amount of bound pepsin per gram of polymer (Q). To mitigate the risk of template bleeding, a blank rebinding experiment was conducted concurrently. In this experiment, each FMIM and FNIM was incubated with 10 mL of double deionised water for the same duration, and the resulting solution was used as a blank for UV measurements of pepsin concentration. The binding capacity Q (mg g-1) was calculated using the following equation:
Q =
(
C i − C t
)
. V / m
where, Ci (mg mL-1) is the initial concentration of pepsin, Ct (mg mL-1) is the concentration of pepsin after incubation time (t), V (mL) is the volume of pepsin solution, and m (g) is the mass of the membranes used in the experiment. The tests were performed three times to ascertain the precision of the results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of the developed FMIM and FNIM
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the developed membranes was investigated via a light microscope to detect any abnormalities and to ensure the uniformity of the surface of the membrane which is an influential parameter in the binding efficiency of the target. Moreover, a fluorescent microscope was employed to visualise the distribution of the CDs into the membrane matrix. This was crucial to ensure that the CDs were uniformly distributed into the membrane matrix. In addition, some pictures were taken for FMIM with and without pepsin under the UV lamp to visualise the fluorescence change upon binding of the target.

To study the thermal behaviour of the developed membranes, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on the FMIMs and FNIMs to determine their thermal stability, decomposition temperature, moisture content, and absence of any residual starting material. TGA was operated in the temperature range (25 to 1000 °C) at a heating rate of 10 °C min-1 and a nitrogen gas flow rate of 50 mL min-1. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was also used to complement the data from the TGA, providing information on moisture content, and melting points of each membrane. DSC was operated at a temperature range from 25 to 500 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C min⁻1 under an inert atmosphere.

The synthesised CDs, FMIM and FNIM were characterised via FTIR to verify the synthesis of the CDs and ensure the incorporation of the CDs into the polymer matrices by comparing the peaks to the reported IR spectra.

To determine the order of binding kinetics, rebinding experiments were conducted on the developed FMIM and FNIM at increasing time intervals. Specifically, each membrane (roughly 150 mg) was incubated with 10 mL of 0.5 mg mL⁻1 pepsin solution for 0, 1, 2, 4, and 6 h. The amount of pepsin adsorbed per gram (Q) for each time interval was plotted against time to determine the binding order kinetics.

Additionally, rebinding experiments were carried out on the developed membranes by applying different concentrations of pepsin to determine the binding isotherm model. For this purpose, each FMIM or FNIM (roughly 150 mg) was incubated with 10 mL of pepsin solutions at concentrations ranging from 0.2 to 1.2 mg mL⁻1 for 4 h. The amount of pepsin adsorbed per gram (Q) for each concentration was plotted against concentration to depict the binding isotherm model.

To verify the binding selectivity of FMIMs, their binding was compared to that of their corresponding FNIMs. The imprinting factor (IF) was then calculated by dividing the value of Q for FMIMs by that for FNIMs. Selectivity of the developed fluorescent membranes against other proteins that may coexist with pepsin in human saliva was also assessed. Amylase and lipase, along with other proteins, were chosen to test selectivity due to their abundance in saliva and potential cross-reactivity. Therefore, a similar binding assay for pepsin was performed on the competitor enzymes, wherein 10 mL of 0.5 mg mL⁻1 solution of each enzyme was incubated with each fluorescent membrane for 4 h. The amount bound per gram (Q) was calculated for each enzyme and compared to that of pepsin.

The CDs used in the synthesis of FMIM were selected due to two important factors. Firstly, the presence of folic acid and amino group residuals on the surface of the produced CDs [28]. This was an essential parameter to ensure the formation of electrostatic interactions between pepsin enzyme and CDs, since, at the working pH of the solution (7), pepsin would be negatively charged due to its low isoelectric point (3.24) and the presence of deprotonated carboxylic groups (pka ∼4) [31], and the amino groups on the surface of CDs would be positively charged (pka ∼9). This guarantees an electrostatic interaction between both that would significantly enhance the binding capacity. Secondly, the ultra-high reported quantum yield (94 %) of these CDs [28] contributes to the better visual detection of the binding between the FMIM and pepsin that can be seen by the naked eye. Figure S1 shows the excitation and emission spectra of the resulting CDs solution.

The quantum yield (Φ) of the synthesised CDs embedded in the FMIM was experimentally determined using quinine sulphate as a standard according to the reported method by Würth et al.[32]. A 0.1 mol L-1 solution of quinine sulphate was prepared in sulphuric acid, which has a known quantum yield of 54 % (Φ = 0.54) at excitation wavelength of 365 nm. This solution was used for comparison.

First, absorbance spectra for both the CDs and the quinine sulphate solutions were recorded at the excitation wavelengths, ensuring that the absorbance values were below 0.1 to minimise inner filter effects. The fluorescence emission spectra were then obtained under identical excitation conditions for both the sample and the standard.

The quantum yield of the CDs was calculated using the following formula:
Φ
u =
Φ
s
×
F u
F s
× A s / A u

Where, Фs and Фu are the quantum yields of quinine and CDs, respectively; Fs and Fu are the integrated fluorescence intensity after correction with the blank for quinine and the CDs, respectively; As and Au are the absorbances at the excitation wavelength of quinine and the CDs, respectively.
The obtained quantum yield value from this experiment was 92.786 % which is very comparable to the reported value in the applied method [28].

The process of preparation of CDs and the fluorescent membranes is shown in Fig. 1 . Different hydrophilic functional monomers were tested in different concentrations with MBA as a crosslinker. Different parameters had to be considered, including, and most importantly, the binding capacity and selectivity. In addition, the mechanical resilience of the resulting membrane, and its ability to retain CDs in its matrix efficiently with no leaching. Table S1 shows the different monomers, crosslinker, and amount of CDs added and the result of the different trials.

From Table S1, we can see that the combination of 1.24 mmol NHMAm and 1.76 mmol Am as monomers, with 0.2 mmol MBA provides the optimum FMIM that has the maximum binding capacity, good mechanical stability, and no leaching of CDs from the membrane's matrix. This can be attributed to the fact that NHMAm and Am can form hydrogen bonds with the amino and carboxylic groups in the backbone of pepsin molecule which reflects positively on the binding capacity. In addition, these monomers can also form hydrogen bonds with the folic acid residues on the surface of CDs preventing their leaching from the membrane. Finally, MBA played a crucial role in the mechanical stability of the resulting membrane, where the low concentration of MBA resulted in a liquid hydrogel like membrane that was not mechanically resilient nor could keep CDs in the membrane's matrix. However, higher concentration of MBA resulted in a semi-solid, glass-like membrane with significantly reduced fluorescence due to the entrapment of CDs in the interior of the membrane. Therefore, the MBA's concentration had to be carefully optimised to reach a satisfactory combination of binding capacity and mechanical resilience.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of the developed FMIM and FNIM

ce:section.ce:para: Images of the FMIM and FNIM captured under a light microscope, as depicted in Fig. 2 a, and b respectively, reveal a uniform, smooth, and wettable surface. This surface morphology significantly enhances the binding capacity of the membrane. A smooth and uniform surface ensures efficient, rapid, and reliable binding and release of the target pepsin enzyme. Wettability further facilitates the application of the FMIM in aqueous matrices. Additionally, fluorescence microscopy images of FMIM, shown in Fig. 2c and d, demonstrate the uniform distribution of CDs within the FMIM's matrix. This uniform distribution is crucial for ensuring that CDs are present at the binding sites to interact with pepsin and generate a visual signal. Moreover, it prevents the occurrence of erroneously high or low fluorescence patches within the FMIM, ensuring consistent performance.

Fig. 3 displays the IR spectra collected for CDs, FMIM and FNIM. FTIR spectra collected for CDs lie in concordance with the reported FTIR spectra for the CDs in the reported method [28]. Herein, a distinct peak is noted at 3274 cm⁻1 that corresponds to the N–H stretching vibration and an asymmetric stretching peak at 1273 cm⁻1 that is attributed to the C–N of the pterin ring. Additionally, the two peaks at 1644 cm⁻1 and 1447 cm⁻1 are attributed to C=O stretching and C–O bending vibrations. The N–H stretching vibration peak of CDs is still observed in the FTIR spectra of both FMIM and FNIM, yet it is slightly shifted to 3486 cm⁻1 due to the presence of an OH group and reduced in intensity due to the incorporation of the CDs in the polymer matrix. In addition to the peaks from CDs, we have a strong peak at 1705 cm⁻1 that corresponds to the stretching of the carbonyl group in the structure of the monomers, and a peak 1359 cm⁻1 that corresponds to the C–H bending. The IR spectra of both FMIM and FNIM are almost identical due to their chemical structural similarity.

TGA curves shown in Fig. 4 a and b, display two weight loss steps: the first around 100 °C due to moisture loss, and the second around 450 °C corresponding to the decomposition of the membrane. In addition, DSC thermograms for FMIM and FNIM shown in Fig. 4c and d exhibit a single strong endothermic peak around 100 °C, corresponding to the high water content of the membrane These findings indicate the high thermal stability of the developed FMIM and FNIM. The similarity between the DSC and TGA curves for FMIM and FNIM further confirms their structural similarity and the absence of any residual target in the FMIM.

The results of the binding kinetics experiments conducted on FMIM and FNIM are graphically illustrated in Fig. 5 a. The graph shows that FMIM exhibits a noticeably enhanced binding capacity compared to FNIM, with the highest binding capacity reached after 4 h. After this period, there is a slight reduction in binding capacity, which can be attributed to the release of pepsin from the binding sites upon reaching equilibrium owing to its bulky structure. Pseudo-first and pseudo-second order kinetics parameters were computed for the experimental data of both FMIM and FNIM, as illustrated in Table 1 . From the presented data, it was concluded that both FMIM and FNIM follow a pseudo-second order kinetics model, suggesting that binding follows a chemical absorption mechanism.

A linear relationship was observed between the binding capacity (Q) and the concentration of pepsin in the binding isotherm experiments conducted on both FMIM and FNIM, as depicted in Fig. 5b. The experimental data were fitted to both the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models to better understand the binding mechanisms. As shown in Table 2 , the data for both FMIM and FNIM fit the Langmuir model better, suggesting that binding occurs in a monolayer form over a homogeneous surface.

The selectivity of FMIM was verified by comparing its binding capacity Q with that of the corresponding FNIM and calculating the imprinting factor (IF) using this formula.
I F =
Q
M I P s
Q
N I P s

At a pepsin concentration of 0.5 mg mL-1, FMIM demonstrated a binding capacity of 21.56 mg g-1, while FNIM showed a value of 8.49 mg g-1, therefore, the IF value was 2.54, providing numerical evidence of FMIM's selectivity.

The results of the selectivity tests conducted against other competitor enzymes, including amylase and lipase, are shown in Figure S2. These results demonstrate the superior selectivity of FMIM for pepsin compared to other enzymes that coexist with pepsin in saliva. The selectivity factor (α) was calculated for each competitor enzyme using this formula.
α =
Q
M I P .
t a r g e t
Q
M I P .
c o m p e t i t o r

The values of α were 5.8 for amylase, 3.17 for lipase, 7.26 for thrombin, and 4.5 for ɣ-globulin. These values confirm that FMIM can bind selectively to pepsin in saliva without noticeable interference from competitor enzymes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on fluorescence response
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value is a crucial parameter that can impact the fluorescence intensity of CDs, thereby affecting the sensitivity of the developed FMIM. Additionally, pH can influence the protein adsorption process by controlling properties of the protein such as ionisation, charge, and conformational integrity. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of FMIM before and after binding the target was studied at different pH values to assess this effect. The results, shown in Fig. 6 , reveal that the fluorescence intensity is highest from pH 5 to pH 7, with the peak at pH 5, consistent with the reported method for synthesising CDs. However, after binding pepsin, the fluorescence intensity is highest at pH 7. This pattern can be explained by the ionisation states of the interacting groups. At lower pH (below 4-5), the carboxylic groups (pka ∼ 4) of pepsin are protonated, reducing the binding interaction between the CDs and pepsin, leading to lower fluorescence. As the pH rises towards neutrality, these groups deprotonate, enhancing electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding between the CDs and pepsin, resulting in a peak fluorescence at pH 7. The peak of fluorescence at pH 7 could be attributed to the optimal interaction between the deprotonated carboxylic groups on pepsin and the amino groups on the CDs, which are not yet fully ionised. Beyond pH 7, the decrease in fluorescence may occur due to potential changes in pepsin's conformation that weaken the interaction with the binding sites in FMIM. Moreover, FMIM synthesis was conducted at pH 7, making the binding sites complementary to the conformation of pepsin at this pH, which is also within the physiological pH range of saliva (6 – 8) [33], the target matrix. Therefore, to achieve the highest possible fluorescence and optimal pepsin binding, pH 7 was chosen as the optimum pH for all the binding experiments.

The increase in fluorescence of FMIM upon binding pepsin can be explained by several factors related to the interaction between pepsin and the CDs in the matrix of the FMIM. Firstly, when pepsin binds to the binding sites containing CDs, it may effectively passivate non-radiative recombination sites on the surface of the CDs. This reduction in non-radiative pathways results in increased fluorescence emission [34,35]. Additionally, the binding of pepsin to the folic acid residues on the surface of the CDs can induce conformational changes that stabilise the structure of the CDs, reducing quenching mechanisms and enhancing fluorescence [36]. Finally, the specific binding of pepsin to the imprinted sites can create a favourable environment for energy transfer mechanisms. Since pepsin binding can facilitate efficient energy transfer to the fluorescent centres of the CDs, it can lead to enhanced fluorescence [37].

The developed FMIMs and FNIMs were evaluated for their ability to visually enhance fluorescence upon binding with pepsin in both solution and spiked saliva samples. The FMIM was viewed under UV light in absence and the presence of pepsin (0.5 mg mL-1) both in solution and in spiked saliva samples. The samples were prepared in phosphate buffer (pH 7) and the visualisation was at room temperature. As depicted in Figure S3, there is a marked increase in fluorescence intensity of the FMIM and FNIM after binding with pepsin, indicating successful interaction. The tested concentration in solution in this qualitative experiment was 0.5 mg mL-1, the FMIM, continued to show a visible fluorescent signal until the concentration of 0.1 mg mL-1, after which the signal did not visibly change. While the visual fluorescence signal becomes apparent at higher pepsin concentrations, this sensitivity still supports the effectiveness of FMIMs as a biosensor. For lower concentrations or precise quantification, further measurements can be accurately performed using spectrofluorometers equipped with a membrane and film solid samples holder. This dual capability highlights the potential of FMIMs for both qualitative and quantitative analysis of pepsin, making it a versatile tool for diagnostic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of this method with other reported MIMs
ce:section.ce:para: To the best of our knowledge, this work was the first to describe the synthesis of a new FMIM for the detection of pepsin in saliva as a biomarker for GERD. Table 3 shows a comparison between the developed FMIM and other reported molecularly imprinted membranes, highlighting the advantages in this work.

In this work, a new, selective, and cost effective fluorescent molecularly imprinted membrane (FMIM) was developed for the binding of pepsin enzyme. Different monomers were tested to achieve the best binding along with the best mechanical stability. The developed FMIM exhibits very high selectivity to pepsin in comparison to fluorescent non-imprinted polymer (FNIM) and to the binding to other competitor enzymes. Carbon dots with high fluorescence intensity were integrated in the membrane matrix to add a fluorescent nature to the membrane making it an ideal visual sensor for pepsin. Thus, binding of pepsin to the FMIM resulted in a visible increase in fluorescence. The developed FMIM and FNIM were characterised meticulously via different analytical techniques to understand more about their nature, stability, and binding behaviour. Finally, the developed FMIM and FNIMs successfully produced a visual response upon binding of pepsin in solutions and spiked saliva samples. This study provides a proof of concept that FMIM is successful in the qualitative detection of pepsin. Future work will aim at applying the biosensor for quantitative determination of pepsin in real patients.

Aya M. Mostafa: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Stephen J. Barton: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. Stephen P. Wren: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. James Barker: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

Aya M. Mostafa reports financial support was provided by Newton Moshrafa scholarship. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the Egyptian Ministry of Higher Education & Scientific Research and The British Council (Newton-Mosharafa Fund) represented by The Egyptian Bureau for Cultural and Educational Affairs in London for their support to this research.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Miss Aya Mohamed is an Assistant Lecturer in the Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department at the Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Egypt. She is currently pursuing her PhD at Kingston University London, UK, under the prestigious Newton Moshrafa scholarship. Miss Mohamed graduated with a degree in Pharmacy with Hons. from Assiut University and holds an MSc in Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry from the same institution. Her research focuses on analytical method development and chromatographic methods, with a recent emphasis on the application of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) as extraction methods and biosensors.

Dr Stephen J Barton is Associate Professor in Analytical Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Analysis in the School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy and Chemistry at Kingston University, London, UK. His research interests include synthesis and analysis of novel polymers and polymer blends for pharmaceutical and environmental applications.

Dr Stephen P. Wren is a Senior Lecturer in Organic & Pharmaceutical Chemistry in the School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy and Chemistry, Kingston University, London, UK. His research interests centre on novel chemical and biosensors, and translational drug discovery.

Prof James Barker is Professor of Analytical Sciences in the School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy and Chemistry, Kingston University, London, UK. He has interests in the development of bio-analytical methods in medicine.
